steps.title: Find the critical constraint
steps.content: The passage provides a specific statistical threshold: a handedness study requires a minimum of 176 individuals to produce a representative result. Both Lockard's and Goodall's studies used only 8 individuals each—far below this threshold.

steps.title: Apply the constraint to the choices
steps.content:
When both studies fall short of the required sample size, neither can reliably yield representative results about handedness.

Choice C directly states this problem: neither study provides sufficient evidence for a meaningful comparison.

Choices A and D both treat at least one study as reliable despite its size (contradicting the 176-person standard), and B assumes Goodall's study is large enough to draw—even mistaken—conclusions from it.

This matches choice C — C is correct.
